hub_subtitle: Conversação para viagens, listening de verdade e reuniões internacionais — rumo a Nova York, setembro de 2026

lesson.menu_desc: Decode fast American English in stores and markets: connected speech, reductions, and the art of listening for keywords instead of every word. Read a strategy guide, hear a fast clerk, and learn to slow the conversation down. The words of real listening (reduction, contraction, connected speech, intonation, word stress, inference, to get the gist, to tune in, to tune out, to catch on, to pick up, to lose the thread) and the grammar that tells your learning story: 'used to + infinitive' -- 'I used to freeze when people spoke fast, but now I listen for keywords.' -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 20 -- Understanding Real English
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucimara | Lesson 20 -- Understanding Real English

listenings.text: Hey there, welcome in, welcome in! Lemme grab that for ya. So, we gotta huge sale goin on today, everything on that back wall is thirty percent off. Whatcha looking for, shirts, jackets? If ya wanna try somethin on, the fitting rooms are right over there. I dunno if we got your size out here, but lemme check in the back real quick. And hey, if it doesn't fit, ya can bring it back, no problem, just hold onto your receipt. Kinda busy today, huh? Alright, gimme one sec and I'll be right with ya.

I want to tell you how my listening changed. I used to panic the moment someone spoke fast. I used to try to translate every single word in my head, and of course I always lost the thread. Honestly, I used to tune out completely and just smile and nod. But little by little, something changed. Now I listen for keywords. I tune in to the important words, and I let the rest go. I get the gist first, and the details come later. Reductions like gonna, wanna, and gotta used to confuse me, but now my ear catches on. The funny thing is, I never sat down to study them. I just picked them up by listening. So if fast English still feels like a wall of noise, be patient. What used to sound impossible slowly starts to make sense.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
a shortened, relaxed form of words in fast speech, like 'gonna' for 'going to'
b
two words joined and shortened, like 'don't' or 'let's'
c
the way words blend together when people talk quickly
d
the rise and fall of the voice that adds meaning
e
the syllable you say louder and longer in a word
f
a smart guess about meaning based on clues and context
g
to understand the general meaning without catching every word
h
to start listening closely and follow what is said
i
to stop paying attention to a sound or a voice
j
to begin to understand something after a moment
k
to learn words informally, just by hearing them
l
to stop following what someone is saying

reading.rtitle: Cracking the Code of Fast English
paras: Real American English does not sound like a textbook. In stores and markets, people link their words together and cut them short. 'What are you looking for' becomes 'whatcha lookin for', and 'let me' becomes 'lemme'. This is called connected speech, and it is full of reductions and contractions. If you stop to understand every single word, you fall behind and lose the thread of the conversation.

Many learners used to freeze at the first fast sentence. They used to try to translate every word in their head, and they used to tune out when it got hard. The real trick is to change that habit. Instead of chasing every sound, you listen for keywords and get the gist. Intonation and a few important words carry most of the meaning, and inference fills in the rest.

With practice, your ear starts to catch on. You tune in to the words that matter and tune out the noise. You pick up new phrases without ever studying them. What used to sound like a wall of noise slowly becomes a real conversation, and a busy New York market stops being scary.
reading.source: Adapted from an American English listening guide

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: You must understand every single word to follow fast American English.
Real American English uses connected speech, so the smart strategy is to listen for keywords and get the gist instead of translating every word.
American markets are too fast for any learner to understand.

items: In connected speech, Americans link their words together and cut them short.
In stores and markets, people link their words together and cut them short.
The text says you should translate every single word in your head.
Instead of chasing every sound, you listen for keywords and get the gist.
Listening for keywords helps you get the gist of a conversation.
Instead of chasing every sound, you listen for keywords and get the gist.
The text says fast English always stays a wall of noise.
What used to sound like a wall of noise slowly becomes a real conversation.

, so words sound shorter and blend together in

, listen for the important ones, and get the
. With practice, your ear will catch

gapfill.text: 'Used to + base verb' describes a past habit or state that is no longer true: 'I used to freeze when people spoke fast.' In the negative and the question, drop the 'd': 'I didn't use to understand', 'Did you use to translate every word?' It always talks about the past, never the present.

bank.label: Useful language for real listening
items: Sorry, could you say that a little slower?
I didn't catch that. One more time, please?
I used to translate every word, but now I listen for the gist.
Do you have this in a smaller size?
I used to freeze, but now I focus on keywords.

lf.title: Complete with 'used to' or 'use to'
items: to freeze when people spoke fast.
to lose the thread all the time.

lf.text: 'Used to + base verb' = a past habit that changed. The affirmative keeps the 'd' (used to); the negative and question drop it (didn't use to, Did you use to?).

items.situation: Tell me one thing about your English you used to do but do not do now.
tips: 'I used to translate every word, but now I listen for keywords.'
used to + base verb = a past habit.

items.situation: A clerk speaks too fast and you miss it. What do you say?
tips: 'Sorry, could you say that a little slower?'
Or: 'I didn't catch that. One more time, please?'
Stay calm and ask, do not freeze.

items.situation: You only caught two words: 'sale' and 'today'. Say the gist.
tips: 'I think there is a sale today.'
You do not need every word to get the gist.

items.situation: Describe how you used to feel in a noisy store, and how you feel now.
tips: 'I used to panic, but now I tune in to keywords.'
Use 'used to' for the past and the present simple for now.
Mention one strategy that helps you.

items.situation: You want to try a shirt in a different size. Ask the clerk.
tips: 'Do you have this in a smaller size?'
A rising intonation makes it a polite question.

items: Tell the class how your listening used to be and how it is now, using 'used to' at least twice.
A fast clerk greets you with 'Whatcha looking for today?'. Respond, and use one phrase to slow them down if you need to.
Explain the difference between trying to catch every word and listening for the gist. Give one example.
Imagine a New York market in September 2026. Say what you used to fear and what you will do instead, using 'used to' at least twice.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = reductions, 2 = connected speech, 3 = in, 4 = gist, 5 = on.
Used to + base verb = a past habit that is no longer true. 'I used to freeze when people spoke fast.'
Negative and question drop the 'd': 'didn't use to', 'Did you use to...?'
Strategy: listen for keywords, get the gist, tune in to what matters, tune out the noise.
answerkey.note: Used to = past habit that changed. Affirmative 'used to', negative/question 'use to'.
